my_sentence: At first I thought they will speak English. However, they speak Japanese so I can't understand all.
correction: At first I thought they would speak English. However, they spoke Japanese, so I couldn't understand it.
explanation: When the main clause is in the past ('I thought'), the verbs that follow shift back too: 'will' becomes 'would', and present 'speak / can't' become past 'spoke / couldn't'. Keep the whole sentence in the past when narrating a past event.
correct_examples: I thought it would be sunny, but it rained, so I couldn't go out.
She said she would call, but she never did.
context: Telling the teacher about joining an AI meeting that turned out to be in Japanese

my_sentence: I don't forget. I think I don't forget the uses of a comma.
correction: I didn't forget the uses of a comma.
explanation: The teacher was asking about a past action (whether you forgot earlier), so the past tense 'didn't forget' is needed, not the present 'don't forget'.
correct_examples: I didn't forget your birthday.
Don't worry, I didn't forget to lock the door.
context: Teacher asking why there was a comma in the sentence

my_sentence: When I came to home, I complain.
correction: When I come home, I complain.
explanation: 'Home' used as a destination is an adverb, so it takes no preposition: say 'come home' / 'go home', never 'come to home'. (Also watch the tense: 'come' for a habitual present, 'came' only for a past event.)
correct_examples: When I come home, I usually cook dinner.
He went home early yesterday.

my_sentence: It's very tired for me to finish this project by myself.
correction: It's very tiring for me to finish this project by myself.
explanation: Use the '-ing' adjective for what causes the feeling and the '-ed' adjective for the person who feels it. The project causes the feeling, so it is 'tiring'; you are 'tired'.
correct_examples: The trip was tiring, so I was tired afterwards.
The lecture was boring and I felt bored.

my_sentence: If I can choose, I like to stay at home to finish it.
correction: If I could choose, I would like to stay at home to finish them.
explanation: For a hypothetical situation, use the second conditional: 'if + past tense' in the condition and 'would + verb' in the result ('If I could choose, I would like ...').
correct_examples: If I could choose, I would work from home.
If I had more time, I would learn the piano.
context: Complaining about having to stay in the office

my_sentence: We had to stay in the office to finish our tasks. That makes me feel terrible.
correction: We had to stay in the office to finish our tasks. That made me feel terrible.
explanation: When the story is set in the past ('had to stay'), the following verb must also be past: 'that made me feel', not 'that makes me feel'. This recurred today (e.g. 'that make me feel impatient' -> 'that made me feel impatient').
correct_examples: I missed the bus, and that made me late.
They cancelled the trip, which made everyone upset.
context: Complaining about working overtime in the office

my_sentence: I lost my wallet when I rode the bus to go home, so I hated the thief steal my wallet.
correction: I lost my wallet when I rode the bus home, so I hated the thief who had stolen it.
explanation: You need a relative pronoun ('who') plus a finite verb to attach the clause to 'the thief', and the action happened before you hated him, so use the past perfect 'had stolen'. Don't repeat 'my wallet'; replace it with the pronoun 'it'.
correct_examples: I thanked the man who had found my keys.
She blamed the driver who had hit her car.
context: Expressing resentment about losing a wallet on the bus

my_sentence: I attended an appointment with my coworker, I felt so angry. That's because he was late.
correction: I attended an appointment with my coworker last week and I was so angry towards him because he arrived late and he didn't inform me about it.
explanation: Don't leave a clause hanging and start a new fragment with 'That's because'. Join the ideas into one sentence with conjunctions ('and', 'because') so the reason connects directly to the main clause.
correct_examples: I was angry because he arrived late and didn't tell me why.
I felt tired, so I went to bed early.
context: Complaining about a coworker who was late to a meeting

my_sentence: He arrived late and he didn't give me any message.
correction: He arrived late and he didn't inform me about it.
explanation: 'Inform me about it' (or 'let me know') is the natural way to say someone failed to tell you in advance; 'give me any message' is L1-influenced and sounds unnatural here.
correct_examples: Please inform me about any changes to the schedule.
He left without letting me know.
context: Complaining about a coworker who was late to a meeting

my_sentence: Doing repetitive tasks every day is boring, so it makes us feel despair.
correction: Doing repetitive tasks every day is boring, so it makes us feel desperate.
explanation: After 'feel' you need an adjective. 'Despair' is a noun, so use the adjective 'desperate' ('feel desperate'). You'd only say 'feel despair' as 'feel a sense of despair'.
correct_examples: The endless work made us feel desperate.
She felt hopeless about the situation.
context: Giving an opinion about repetitive jobs using gerund phrases

my_sentence: Being a pilot, it is dangerous.
explanation: When a gerund phrase ('Being a pilot') is the subject, don't add a second subject pronoun 'it' afterwards. The gerund phrase already is the subject. This recurred (e.g. 'Being a doctor, it's not good' -> 'Being a doctor is not good').
correct_examples: Being a pilot is stressful because it requires a lot of focus.
Learning a language takes time.
context: Giving opinions about jobs using gerund phrases

my_sentence: It can help us to fix many problems.
correction: It can help us fix many problems.
explanation: The causative-style verbs 'help', 'let', and 'make' are followed by a bare infinitive (no 'to'): 'help us fix', 'let you do', 'make me feel'. Today both 'help us to fix' and 'let you to do' had the extra 'to'.
correct_examples: It helps us solve problems faster.
My boss lets me work from home.
context: Giving an opinion about being a computer engineer

my_sentence: Working as a computer engineer is awesome. It allowed you to solve some technical problems.
correction: Working as a computer engineer is awesome. It allows you to solve some technical problems.
explanation: When you state a general opinion in the present tense, keep every verb in the present: 'it allows', not 'it allowed'. Don't switch to the past for a fact that is still true.
correct_examples: This job is great because it allows you to travel.
Exercise helps you stay healthy.
context: Giving an opinion about being a computer engineer

my_sentence: As a scientist, you can use your abilities to help human.
correction: As a scientist, you can use your abilities to help humans.
explanation: To talk about people in general, use the plural 'humans' (or 'people'). Singular 'human' needs an article ('a human'), which changes the meaning to one person.
correct_examples: Scientists work to help humans live longer.
Robots are built to help people with daily tasks.
context: Giving an opinion about being a scientist

my_sentence: A person can earn money by him or her.
correction: A person can earn money by himself or herself.
explanation: After 'by' meaning 'alone / without help', use the reflexive pronoun 'himself / herself', not the object pronoun 'him / her'.
correct_examples: She finished the project by herself.
He can do it by himself.
context: Discussing the pros of having a job

my_sentence: We can use our time to help other fix their problems.
correction: We can use our time to help others fix their problems.
explanation: As a pronoun meaning 'other people', use the plural 'others'. 'Other' on its own is a determiner that needs a noun after it ('other people').
correct_examples: We should help others when we can.
Some people stayed; others left early.
context: Discussing how having a job helps society

my_sentence: Choosing to be a doctor is not a good career that takes a lot of time.
correction: Choosing to be a doctor is not a good career because it takes a lot of time and you can't earn enough money.
explanation: Don't drop a relative 'that' into the middle of a complete clause; it pulls the focus onto the relative clause and away from your main point. Use a conjunction like 'because' to add the reason instead. (assistant note: the teacher flagged the mid-sentence 'that' but the final wording is reconstructed.)
correct_examples: It isn't a good career because it pays little.
I dropped the class because it took too much time.
context: Giving an opinion about being a doctor using gerund phrases
